categories.title: Introduction to SignWriting
categories.description: Learn the fundamentals of the visual writing system for sign languages

lessons.title: What is SignWriting?
lessons.summary: Discover the visual writing system created by Valerie Sutton to represent sign languages.
objectives: Learn the history of the system

sections.title: What is SignWriting?
sections.body: SignWriting is a visual writing system created by Valerie Sutton in 1974. It allows graphically representing hand movements, facial expressions and body postures used in sign languages.
bullets: Represents any sign language in the world
sections.highlight: SignWriting is the only writing system capable of representing any sign language in the world!

exercises.prompt: In what year was SignWriting created?
exercises.explanation: SignWriting was created by Valerie Sutton in 1974 as part of her movement notation system.

exercises.prompt: SignWriting can only be used for American Sign Language (ASL).
exercises.explanation: SignWriting can represent any sign language in the world and is used in over 40 countries.

categories.title: Reading SignWriting
categories.description: Practice how to read handshapes, movement, contact and facial symbols

lessons.title: The visual map of a sign
lessons.summary: Learn how SignWriting arranges symbols as a visual map instead of a linear word.
objectives: Recognize the visual area of a written sign
Connect symbol placement with body location
Read the whole sign before individual details

sections.title: Read the whole image first
sections.body: A written sign is organized visually. Hand symbols, movement arrows, facial symbols and body information appear in relation to each other.
bullets: Higher symbols often relate to upper body locations
Left and right placement help show spatial relationships
Movement arrows should be read with the symbol that moves
The full layout gives context before details
sections.highlight: Start with the overall shape of the sign, then inspect each symbol.

sections.title: From overview to detail
sections.body: A reliable reading routine checks hands, location, movement and expression in sequence.
bullets: Check where the sign is located

exercises.prompt: What is a good first step when reading a written sign?

options.label: Read it like a spoken-language word

exercises.explanation: SignWriting is visual, so the complete layout gives important context.

exercises.prompt: Symbol placement can help show where the sign happens in the body or signing space.
exercises.explanation: Placement is part of the visual information in a written sign.

lessons.title: Handshape and orientation
lessons.summary: Compare hand symbols and learn how palm direction changes the reading.

sections.title: Small details matter
sections.body: Two signs can look similar but differ by finger position or palm orientation. Careful comparison makes reading more accurate.
bullets: Notice whether fingers are together or separated
Compare open hand, fist and curved fingers
Look for the direction of the palm
sections.highlight: A small change in handshape can change the sign.

sections.title: A comparison routine
sections.body: When symbols look close, compare one feature at a time: fingers, thumb, palm, location and movement.

exercises.prompt: Which feature should you check when comparing hand symbols?
exercises.explanation: Handshape and orientation are core visual details.

exercises.prompt: Similar hand symbols always represent the same sign.
exercises.explanation: Similar symbols may differ in important details.

lessons.title: Movement arrows
lessons.summary: Understand how arrows show direction, repetition and movement type.
objectives: Recognize the purpose of movement arrows

sections.title: What arrows show
sections.body: Movement arrows tell you where a hand or body part moves. They can show direction, repeated movement and movement path.
bullets: The arrow tip points toward the movement direction
Repeated arrows can show repeated movement
The arrow belongs to a hand, face or body symbol
Direction must be read in the sign layout
sections.highlight: Do not read the arrow alone; connect it to what is moving.

sections.title: Read movement in context
sections.body: Look for where the movement starts, where it ends and whether contact or repetition appears.

exercises.prompt: The tip of a movement arrow usually indicates:
exercises.explanation: Arrows guide the direction of movement in the written sign.

exercises.prompt: A movement arrow should be connected to the symbol that moves.
exercises.explanation: The arrow is meaningful because it describes movement of a specific symbol.

lessons.title: Contact and facial expression
lessons.summary: Learn how touch, proximity and facial information improve the written sign.
objectives: Understand facial symbols as language information

sections.title: Touch is meaningful
sections.body: Some signs touch the face, torso or another hand. SignWriting can represent contact so the sign can be read more precisely.
bullets: Contact may change the meaning of a sign
Touch and near movement are different
The contact point helps locate the sign
sections.highlight: Contact is linguistic information, not decoration.

sections.title: Face and body
sections.body: Facial expression, eye gaze and body posture can mark questions, negation, intensity or focus.
bullets: Mouth movement may add intensity or shape
Body posture can clarify the message

exercises.prompt: Why is contact information useful?

options.label: It shows whether the sign touches or approaches a location

exercises.explanation: Contact helps readers understand how the sign happens in space or on the body.

exercises.prompt: Facial expression can carry grammar in sign languages.
exercises.explanation: Facial expression can mark questions, negation, intensity and other grammatical information.

categories.title: Libras Foundations
categories.description: Build basic Brazilian Sign Language skills for everyday communication

lessons.title: Greetings and polite interaction
lessons.summary: Practice simple greetings, attention and polite visual interaction in Libras.
objectives: Understand the role of friendly expression

sections.title: Start with visual attention
sections.body: In sign language interaction, visual attention matters. A greeting works best when the other person is ready to see the sign.
bullets: Make sure the person can see you
Pause for the other person's response
sections.highlight: Polite interaction starts with shared visual attention.

sections.title: Short exchanges
sections.body: A simple exchange can include greeting, name, question and closing. Keep each part clear before adding more information.

exercises.prompt: What should you confirm before starting a signed greeting?

options.label: That the person can visually attend to you
That the room is completely silent

exercises.explanation: Sign language interaction depends on shared visual attention.

exercises.prompt: A friendly facial expression can support a greeting in Libras.
exercises.explanation: Expression is part of clear and respectful visual communication.

lessons.title: Fingerspelling in context
lessons.summary: Learn when fingerspelling is useful and how to keep it readable.
objectives: Use fingerspelling for names and specific terms

sections.title: When to fingerspell
sections.body: Fingerspelling is useful for names, acronyms and terms without a known sign. It should support communication, not replace the whole language.
bullets: Use it for acronyms or specific terms
Use the known sign when available
sections.highlight: Fingerspelling is a tool, not the whole language.

sections.title: Rhythm and clarity
sections.body: A steady rhythm is easier to read than very fast fingerspelling. Keep your hand comfortable and letters clear.
bullets: Check if the other person understood

exercises.prompt: Fingerspelling is especially useful for:
exercises.explanation: Fingerspelling supports communication when a specific word must be spelled.

exercises.prompt: Fingerspelling too quickly can make comprehension harder.
exercises.explanation: Clear rhythm helps the other person read each letter.

lessons.title: Using signing space
lessons.summary: See how space can organize people, places and ideas in Libras.
objectives: Use reference points in signing space
Use gaze and body orientation clearly

sections.title: Space organizes meaning
sections.body: Libras can use points in front of the body to represent people, places or ideas. Gaze and body orientation help return to those points.
bullets: A point can represent a person
Eye gaze can return to that point
Body orientation can show a shift in focus
sections.highlight: Space is part of the structure of the language.

sections.title: Stay consistent
sections.body: After assigning a reference to a location, keep the same logic while the topic continues.
bullets: Choose a point for each reference
Return to it with gaze or pointing
Use body shifts when the focus changes

exercises.prompt: Signing space can help organize:
exercises.explanation: Spatial organization is a core visual resource in sign languages.

exercises.prompt: Keeping spatial references consistent helps the conversation stay clear.
exercises.explanation: Consistency helps the viewer track who or what is being discussed.

lessons.title: Questions and negation
lessons.summary: Practice visual markers for questions, answers and negation.

sections.title: Visual questions
sections.body: Questions can involve facial expression, gaze and question signs. The face helps the viewer understand the intent of the sentence.
bullets: Keep the expression through the question
End with attention to the viewer
sections.highlight: Facial expression should match the question intent.

sections.title: Negation
sections.body: Negation may combine manual signs, head movement and facial expression. These elements often work together.
bullets: Facial expression shows disagreement or impossibility
Negation signs need the right context
Clarity comes from the whole sentence

exercises.prompt: Which element often helps mark questions in Libras?
exercises.explanation: Face and gaze are important visual markers in signed questions.

exercises.prompt: Negation can include facial expression and head movement.
exercises.explanation: Negation may combine manual, facial and body resources.

categories.title: Guided Practice
categories.description: Short practice lessons for observation, dialogue and review

lessons.title: Visual observation routine
lessons.summary: Train yourself to observe signs through hands, movement, face and body.
objectives: Notice details without losing the whole sign

sections.title: Observe in layers
sections.body: First look at the overall sign. Then focus on handshape, movement, face and body one layer at a time.
sections.highlight: Layered observation helps you catch more details.

sections.title: Build visual memory
sections.body: After observing a sign, pause and try to remember its overall shape before checking it again.
bullets: Compare your memory with the original sign

exercises.prompt: What does layered observation mean?

options.label: Checking the whole sign, hands, movement, face and body

exercises.explanation: Layered observation improves awareness of all sign components.

exercises.prompt: Face and body should be part of sign observation practice.
exercises.explanation: Sign languages use the full visual body space.

lessons.title: Build short dialogues
lessons.summary: Combine greeting, question, answer and closing in simple interactions.
objectives: Combine signs with attention and expression

sections.title: Beginning, middle and end
sections.body: A short dialogue can include opening, a question or main information, a response and closing. Visual attention helps organize turns.
sections.highlight: Eye contact and pauses make turn-taking clearer.

sections.title: Simple responses
sections.body: Practice short responses before longer conversations. Clear answers build confidence.
bullets: Avoid adding too much information at first
Repeat the same dialogue with variations

exercises.prompt: What helps organize turn-taking in a signed dialogue?
exercises.explanation: Visual attention and pauses support clear interaction.

exercises.prompt: A short dialogue can start with a greeting, include a question and end with a closing.
exercises.explanation: This structure is useful for everyday practice.

lessons.title: Review mistakes
lessons.summary: Use missed questions as a guide for what to study next.
objectives: Practice the same topic again with focus

sections.title: Mistakes show the next step
sections.body: A wrong answer is useful feedback. It can show whether you need to review handshape, movement, expression or meaning.
bullets: Check the explanation after each answer
Write down the topic you missed
Return to the related lesson section
sections.highlight: Reviewing mistakes is part of practice, not a failure.

sections.title: Look for patterns
sections.body: If several mistakes involve the same topic, make that topic your next study focus.
bullets: Repeated handshape errors need comparison practice
Expression errors need face and body observation

exercises.prompt: What should you do after missing a question?

options.label: Read the explanation and review the related topic

exercises.explanation: Mistakes point to what needs focused review.

exercises.prompt: Repeated mistakes can reveal a topic that needs more practice.
exercises.explanation: Patterns in mistakes are useful for planning the next review.

lessons.title: Final basics review
lessons.summary: Review the core ideas before moving into more advanced content.

sections.title: Core elements
sections.body: The basics include handshape, orientation, movement, face, body and signing space. These elements support both Libras learning and SignWriting reading.
bullets: Space organizes people, places and ideas
sections.highlight: Strong basics make the next lessons easier.

sections.title: How to continue
sections.body: Keep alternating lesson reading, exercises, review and real sign observation whenever possible.
bullets: Write down questions for later study

exercises.prompt: Which elements are part of the visual basics?

options.label: Hands, movement, face, body and space

exercises.explanation: These elements form the base of sign language observation and SignWriting reading.

exercises.prompt: Reviewing lessons with low scores helps consolidate learning.
exercises.explanation: Focused review strengthens the topics that are still difficult.
